title: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System
items.title: Classification of Respiratory Diseases by Pathophysiology
items.content: Respiratory diseases are categorized into obstructive, restrictive, and vascular based on their pathophysiological mechanisms. Obstructive diseases involve airway obstruction (e.g., asthma, COPD), restrictive diseases involve reduced lung expansion (e.g., parenchymal lung disease), and vascular diseases affect the pulmonary vasculature (e.g., pulmonary embolism).
keyPoints: Obstructive pathophysiology is most common.
Examples of obstructive diseases: asthma, COPD, bronchiectasis, bronchiolitis.
Restrictive diseases include parenchymal lung disease, chest wall abnormalities, and neuromuscular disease.
Vascular disorders examples: pulmonary embolism, pulmonary hypertension.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System
items.title: Initial Evaluation of Respiratory Disorders
items.content: The evaluation of respiratory system disorders begins with a thorough history and focused physical examination. This is followed by diagnostic tests such as pulmonary function testing, chest imaging, blood and sputum analysis, serologic or microbiologic studies, and procedures like bronchoscopy.
keyPoints: Start with a thorough history and physical examination.
Common subsequent investigations: pulmonary function tests, chest imaging, blood/sputum analysis.
Additional diagnostic procedures may include bronchoscopy.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.title: Characterizing Dyspnea for Etiological Clues
items.content: The description and onset of dyspnea can guide the clinician toward specific etiologies. Obstructive lung diseases often present with 'chest tightness' or difficulty in deep breathing, while congestive heart failure is associated with sensations of 'air hunger' or suffocation.
keyPoints: Obstructive lung disease: 'chest tightness', 'inability to get a deep breath'
Congestive heart failure: 'air hunger', sense of 'suffocation'
Acute dyspnea: associated with sudden physiologic changes like airway narrowing, acute hypoxemia, or pneumothorax
COPD and IPF: gradual progression of dyspnea on exertion with acute exacerbations
Asthma: intermittent episodes triggered by specific factors
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Understanding Cough Characteristics for Diagnosis
items.content: The duration, sputum production, and triggers of a cough can indicate its underlying cause. Acute productive coughs often suggest respiratory infections, while chronic coughs may point to obstructive lung diseases or non-respiratory conditions like gastroesophageal reflux.
keyPoints: Acute cough with phlegm: suggests infection (e.g., sinusitis, bronchitis)
Chronic cough (>8 weeks): associated with asthma, COPD, bronchiectasis
Nonproductive chronic cough: may indicate IPF or other diffuse parenchymal lung diseases
Hemoptysis: requires urgent evaluation
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Assessing Additional Symptoms and Social History in Respiratory Disease
items.content: Additional symptoms like wheezing, hemoptysis, chest pain, and signs of cor pulmonale can provide further diagnostic clues. A comprehensive social history including smoking, vaping, and environmental exposures is crucial for identifying risk factors and potential causes.
keyPoints: Wheezing: suggestive of airways disease (e.g., asthma)
Hemoptysis: may indicate infections, bronchogenic carcinoma, pulmonary embolism
Chest pain from respiratory disorders: often due to pleural or vascular diseases
Social history: smoking and vaping are risk factors for many respiratory diseases
Environmental exposures: occupational or home-related inhalational risks
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.title: Identifying Infective Etiologies in Respiratory Symptoms
items.content: Fever and chills should prompt consideration of infectious causes, both pulmonary and systemic. A comprehensive review can reveal rheumatologic or autoimmune diseases presenting with respiratory symptoms.
keyPoints: Associated symptoms of fever and chills suggest infective etiologies.
Review systems for joint pain, rashes, dry eyes/mouth, constitutional symptoms to identify rheumatologic/autoimmune disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Respiratory Distress and Impairment
items.content: Physical examination begins with vital signs. Tachypnea or hypopnea, along with pulse oximetry indicating hypoxemia, are critical indicators of respiratory disease.
keyPoints: Respiratory rate (tachypnea/hypopnea) and pulse oximetry are key initial assessments.
Inability to complete a sentence suggests severe impairment.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.title: Distinguishing Respiratory Sounds
items.content: Auscultation reveals abnormalities like wheezes, rhonchi, and crackles. Wheezes indicate airway obstruction; rhonchi suggest medium-sized airway obstruction with secretions; crackles are associated with alveolar disease.
keyPoints: Wheezes: airway obstruction (e.g., asthma, bronchogenic carcinoma).
Rhonchi: medium-sized airway obstruction, often with secretions.
Crackles: alveolar disease (e.g., pulmonary edema, pneumonia).
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.title: Recognizing Red Flags in Respiratory Disease History
items.content: In patients with respiratory symptoms, it is crucial to identify red flags that may indicate serious underlying conditions. Clubbing and cyanosis are key physical findings associated with significant lung diseases.
keyPoints: Clubbing can be found in many lung diseases including cystic fibrosis, IPF, and lung cancer.
Cyanosis occurs in hypoxemic respiratory disorders resulting in >5 g of deoxygenated hemoglobin/dL.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 3) | DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.title: Interpreting Spirometry for Airflow Obstruction and Restriction
items.content: Spirometry is the initial pulmonary function test used to differentiate between obstructive and restrictive lung diseases. Key metrics include FEV1/FVC ratio and total lung capacity.
keyPoints: A diminished FEV1/FVC ratio (<70%) is diagnostic of airflow obstruction, seen in conditions like asthma, COPD, and bronchiectasis.
Total lung capacity <80% of predicted value defines restrictive pathophysiology, which can result from parenchymal disease, neuromuscular weakness, or chest wall/pleural diseases.
Restriction with impaired gas exchange (decreased DLCO) suggests parenchymal lung disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 3) | DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System
items.question: Which of the following diseases is NOT typically associated with obstructive pathophysiology?
items.explanation: Pulmonary embolism is associated with vascular diseases, not obstructive pathophysiology.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System
items.question: Which category does neuromuscular disease fall under in terms of respiratory system disorders?
items.explanation: Neuromuscular diseases result in restrictive pathophysiology.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System
items.question: What is the initial step in evaluating a patient with a respiratory system disorder?
options: Thorough history and focused physical examination
items.explanation: The evaluation begins with a thorough history and a focused physical examination.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.question: Which description of dyspnea is most commonly reported by patients with congestive heart failure?
options: "chest tightness" or "inability to get a deep breath"
"air hunger" or a sense of suffocation
gradual progression of dyspnea on exertion
intermittent episodes of dyspnea, cough, and chest tightness
items.explanation: Patients with congestive heart failure commonly report 'air hunger' or a sense of suffocation.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What is the typical duration for defining chronic cough?
items.explanation: Chronic cough is defined as persisting for more than 8 weeks.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: Which condition is NOT typically associated with acute shortness of breath?
items.explanation: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis typically causes a gradual progression of dyspnea, not acute shortness of breath.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: What should be determined when assessing hemoptysis in patients?
options: the urgency for evaluation as delineated in Chap. 41
whether it is associated with wheezing
if it occurs only during exertion
items.explanation: Hemoptysis warrants urgent evaluation as delineated in Chap. 41.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following symptoms should raise suspicion of an infective etiology in a patient with respiratory disease?
items.explanation: Fever and chills are associated symptoms that raise suspicion of infective etiologies.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.question: What should be assessed to help distinguish between crackles due to alveolar fluid and those due to interstitial fibrosis?
items.explanation: Assessing for egophony can help distinguish between crackles associated with alveolar fluid and those due to interstitial fibrosis.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.question: Which physical examination finding is generally a sign of severe respiratory impairment?
options: Inability to complete a sentence in conversation
items.explanation: Inability to complete a sentence in conversation is generally a sign of severe respiratory impairment.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.question: What can chronic rhonchi suggest in the context of respiratory disease?
items.explanation: Chronic rhonchi suggest bronchiectasis or COPD.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.question: Which of the following conditions is NOT typically associated with clubbing?
items.explanation: Clubbing is typically associated with cystic fibrosis, IPF, and lung cancer, but not specifically hypoxemic respiratory disorders.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 3) | DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.question: In the context of acute respiratory symptoms, what is a common approach to diagnostic evaluation?
options: Performing tests in a protracted, stepwise fashion
Evaluating with multiple tests performed simultaneously
Delaying testing until chronic dyspnea develops
items.explanation: Acute respiratory symptoms are often evaluated with multiple tests performed at the same time to diagnose life-threatening diseases rapidly.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 3) | DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.question: What does a diminished FEV1/FVC ratio (<70%) indicate in spirometry?
items.explanation: A diminished FEV1/FVC ratio (<70%) is diagnostic of airflow obstruction.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 3) | DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System
items.statement: All respiratory disorders manifest as gas exchange abnormalities.
items.explanation: Many respiratory disorders do not manifest as gas exchange abnormalities.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System
items.statement: Infective processes can affect the respiratory system and result in pathologic findings.
items.explanation: Infective processes are among those that can affect the respiratory system.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.statement: Dyspnea on exertion is an early symptom of underlying lung or heart disease.
items.explanation: Dyspnea on exertion often indicates early symptoms of underlying lung or heart disease.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Patients with asthma typically experience daily symptoms of dyspnea.
items.explanation: Most asthmatics do not have daily symptoms but experience intermittent episodes associated with specific triggers.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.statement: Wheezes can be both polyphonic and monophonic, depending on the underlying cause.
items.explanation: Wheezes can indeed be polyphonic or monophonic based on whether they involve multiple airways or a single size airway.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.statement: The distinction between 'wet' and 'dry' crackles reliably differentiates among etiologies of respiratory disease.
items.explanation: The distinction between 'wet' and 'dry' crackles has not been shown to be a reliable way to differentiate among etiologies.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.statement: Cyanosis occurs in respiratory disorders that result in more than 5 g/dL of deoxygenated hemoglobin.
items.explanation: Cyanosis is seen in hypoxemic respiratory disorders with >5 g of deoxygenated hemoglobin/dL.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 3) | DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.statement: Spirometry alone can definitively diagnose neuromuscular weakness.
items.explanation: Additional testing, such as measurements of maximal inspiratory and expiratory pressures, is needed to diagnose neuromuscular weakness.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 3) | DIAGNOSTIC EVALUATION

items.subtopic: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System
items.assertion: The majority of diseases of the respiratory system present with cough and/or dyspnea.
items.reason: These symptoms are common presentations in obstructive, restrictive, and vascular diseases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System

items.assertion: Cough productive of phlegm is often a symptom of respiratory infection.
items.reason: Acute cough productive of phlegm can indicate infections affecting the upper or lower airways, or lung parenchyma.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: Chest pain from respiratory disorders is due to innervation of lung parenchyma with pain fibers.
items.reason: Pain in the chest from respiratory disorders usually results from diseases of the parietal pleura or pulmonary vascular diseases.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because lung parenchyma is not innervated with pain fibers; however, the reason is true.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 1)

items.assertion: Stridor is a high-pitched, focal inspiratory wheeze.
items.reason: It is usually heard over the neck as a manifestation of upper airway obstruction.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)

items.assertion: Pulsus paradoxus is an ominous sign in obstructive lung disease.
items.reason: It indicates significant negative intrathoracic pressures required for ventilation and impending respiratory failure.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Approach to the Patient with Disease of the Respiratory System · HISTORY (part 2)
